stonetop.summary: The North Manmarch lies east of the Great Wood , north of the Highway and northeast of Marshedge .

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
The North Manmarch lies east of the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{Great Wood}, north of the Highway and northeast of @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.uXlyry9CpXUz4ooR]{Marshedge}. Its western edge is made up of rolling hills, giving way to a wide, sparsely-wooded prairie that slopes gently towards the east. Many springs bubble up from the hills and flow eastward in tiny streams that feed the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lGNIQWIn8CPHuM7E]{Dread River}.

The region is heavily populated and small, well-armed hamlets dot the hills and plains.

sections.heading: Impressions
sections.body:
Always

  • A wide-open sky, a sense that you can see forever
  • Wagon-rut trails and well-worn foot paths stretching on and on
  • Longhouses with steep, thatched roofs
  • Smell of woodsmoke, dung, wet fur
  • Huge rams pulling plows, wagons
  • Scarred faces, hard glares, weapons at hand even while working fields

Spring

  • Pale wildflowers bobbing in the wind
  • Damp smell of mud or fresh-tilled earth

Summer

  • Baled hay, laid out to bake
  • Sun beating down on you, sweaty clothes sticking to you

Autumn

  • Crop fields, neatly clipped after harvest
  • A startled deer, bounding away

Winter

  • Tracks in the snow: paw prints, hoof prints, foot prints, sled trails, etc.
  • Twinkling stars on a cold night, sure sign of a coming storm

sections.heading: The Manmarchers
sections.body:
Thousands of people dwell here, spread out over hundreds of hamlets. Those hamlets cluster around a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements. These hillforts are home to chiefs who (ostensibly) protect the surrounding hamlets.

Manmarchers say “east of the river” the way others say “once upon a time.” They migrated from across the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lGNIQWIn8CPHuM7E]{Dread River} long ago, before the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.JDCcrvQhMYFFlUIe]{Barrow Builders} called up the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{Things Below} and made the waters unsafe.

sections.heading: Names
sections.body: Germanic-inspired: Alfher, Bathhilde, Berkhard, Bertrim, Clothar, Dagmar, Elfrida, Ganter, Gerhild, Hartig, Hilde, Hiltrude, Hramn, Ludig, Luise, Meike, Modd, Sabrinne, Swanhilde, Ulrike, Urrsla, Weillem, Wiland, Wulfrim

sections.heading: Lore
sections.body:
Everyone has heard of the Manmarchers’ warlike ways. They fight mostly among themselves, the violence fueled by bloodfeuds, treachery, and ambition. The area might see a decade or two of peace, but only after war leaves the best part of a generation dead or maimed.

Something interesting: When one Manmarcher kills or harms another in times of peace, the victim’s family is owed a wergild—coin, livestock, or other wealth. The victim’s chief sets the wergild (usually Value 2, or Value 3 for a person of import). Failure by the accused or their kin to pay is grounds for blood-feud.

Something useful: Scheming chiefs will justify violence by setting an unreasonable or highly specific wergild. Should the wergild be paid, though, the feud must be called off. A paid wergild is sacrosanct—a chief who ignores it invites a curse upon their people.

pairs.prompt: How is the Manmarcher language different from that of **@UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}**?
When did the last round of fighting start among the Manmarchers?
Who all did you lose to the violence?
Which hamlet or hillfort do you hail from?
How does it differ from what’s done in Stonetop?

The Manmarchers fell trees from the **@UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6kt1b8ozEREDCi4k]{Great Wood}**—did they break or just never have a compact with the **@UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.wxslMExj4dQkTPfw]{Forest Folk}**?
How do Manmarchers treat Green Lord ruins and artifacts?
What is this clan best known for?
How can you tell them from other clans?
What tells you that this land has been shaped by human activity, even though there’s not a settlement in sight?

sections.heading: Hooks
sections.body:
• Manmarchers waylay a merchant south of the Highway, stealing something important/dangerous/valuable.
  • News arrives that the kin of a Manmarcher PC (or important NPC) were butchered in the latest round of fighting.
  • People are vanishing from some of the western hamlets, and the local chief seems powerless to stop it.
  • Manmarcher refugees arrive in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}, promising buried treasure to whomever avenges them.
  • An ambitious chief has conquered three of the hillforts, and is preparing to move on a fourth. If they succeed, they will likely unite the Manmarchers, and then what?

sections.heading: Terrain
sections.body:
Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

1d12 terrain

RollResult
1Spring/pond/creek/stream
2Ditch/wash/gully/ravine
3Copse/thicket/grove of trees
4Steep slope/rocky outcrop/large hill
5Gentle slope/low ridge/small hill
6–7Open meadow, grass waist-high or shorter
8Footpath/wagon ruts/trail
9–10Farmland/pasture
11Fence/low wall/embankment
12Hamlet/hillfort/isolated longhouse

sections.heading: Discoveries
sections.body:
For any one of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

1d6 discovery

RollResult
1Change of terrain and roll again
2A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
3signs of violence (see below)
4Beasts: maybe game (deer, turkey, pheasant, etc.) or a grazing herd watched by a youth and some dogs
5Farmers: working the land, hunting, or heading somewhere
6Warriors: not hostile, certainly wary

1d6 signs of violence

RollResult
1A ghost lingering near its murder, more confused than dangerous
2A burnt hamlet, thoroughly looted, its inhabitants slaughtered
3Dead bodies: burnt on a pyre, heads on spikes, swinging from trees, or left to rot
4Survivors of a raid, some injured, likely desperate
5Sounds of a fight, or calls for help
6The dead from a recent skirmish, un-looted except by scavengers

sections.heading: Hamlet
sections.body:
Here’s a typical settlement:

Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls) Population +0 Prosperity -1

Trade with other hamlets Resources (pick 2-3)

  • Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool)
  • Farming (wheat, barley, oats, honey, mead)
  • Timber
  • Trapping (fur, meat)
  • Mining (amber, salt, and/or iron) Defenses +1
  • Warrior culture
  • Iron weapons, wooden shields
  • Protection from the local chief

Hamlets consist of a longhouse for each family and their livestock, plus smaller outbuildings (sheds, cellars, etc.), clustered together. Inhabitants work the surrounding 1-2 miles of land. Each hamlet bears the name of its most notable resident, possibly truncated, followed by “-hall” (Dagmarhall, Swanhall, etc.).

sections.heading: Hillfort
sections.body:
There are about a half-dozen larger, fortified settlements spread across the North Manmarch, each 20-30 miles apart. Most are in the western hills, but a couple sit on the plains to the east, at the western edge of the woodlands that overlook the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lGNIQWIn8CPHuM7E]{Dread River}.

Hillfort names might include: Wulfberg, Rabenberg, Eikelberg, Hokberg, Tiefberg, Baerinberg, Schutberg, etc.

Size village (~150 to 350 souls) Population -1 Prosperity +0

  • Trade with other hillforts
  • Trade with @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.uXlyry9CpXUz4ooR]{Marshedge} and/or @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.DPUvs6Ss8UHXEsCJ]{Lygos} (textile, spices, glass, herbs, finery) Resources (pick 2-3)
  • Taxes from surrounding hamlets
  • Trades: smithing, tanning, pottery
  • Livestock (pigs, goats, sheep, meat, milk, wool)
  • Market (for surrounding hamlets)
  • Timber Defenses +2
  • A dozen or so professional warriors
  • Rotating recruits from the hamlets
  • Iron weapons, wooden shields
  • Series of earthen ramparts
  • Palisades

Each hamlet is bound to one of the hillforts and its chief, through oaths or blood. The chief keeps a standing force of warriors, and is responsible for protecting their hamlets from other clans, or (when that’s not possible) demanding wergild or exacting vengeance.

sections.heading: Trade & Barter
sections.body:
Commonly available The following are special items in @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.6yScslDfqrcCQ6CJ]{Stonetop}, but commonly available in the North Manmarch—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop whisky is usually in demand.

weapons of war value

  • Mace or flail, iron (close, forceful) 1
  • Battleaxe, iron (close, messy) 1
  • Sword, iron (close, +1 damage) 1
  • Warhammer (close, 2 piercing) 1

armor value

Cuirass, boiled leather (1 armor) 1

goods value

  • Salt (a little box) 0 Skin of mead, common 0
  • Firkin of mead, common 0
  • Salt (a pouch full) 1 Bit of amber 1 Barrel of mead, common (immobile) 1 Cartload of timber 2

transport value

Wheelbarrow 1 Cart or sleigh (requires donkey/ 2 horse/mule) Wagon (requires horse/mule) 3 Spare parts for wagon/cart/sleigh (axles, 2 wheels, etc.; uses, immobile)

livestock value

provisions (1d6+10 uses)

Special items The following might be acquired in the North Manmarch, in addition to special items from the Moves & Gear handout. PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them.

When PCs Trade & Barter in the North Manmarch, they use the local Prosperity (-1 and disadvantage for a hamlet, +0 in a hillfort, by default). On a 6-, they don’t mark XP, and if they want to sell/acquire it, they’ll need to talk to the local chief.

goods value

Skin of mead, fine ( uses, share 1 use 0 with someone to gain advantage to Persuade) Firkin of mead, fine 1 Barrel of mead, fine (immobile) 2 Piece of amber, beautifully worked 1 Bit of silver and amber jewelry 2 Large chunk of amber 2 Chariot (requires horse/tonnerhorn) 2

livestock value

Services and favors The PCs don’t need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the locals—even if they offer something of equal value. A likely way to convince them is to bribe the local elder, or get permission/backing from the local chief.

A hamlet that’s well-disposed to the PCs might grant these favors for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price.

service value

A night’s food and shelter 0 A guide to the next hamlet 0 A laborer’s help for a day 0 A crew of laborers, for a week 1 An armed escort for a couple days 1 A warrior to join you on a nice 1 simple raid A small crew, for a week or two of action 2 Someone to hide you from vengeance 2 for a few days A crew of toughs for a season of action 3 A fitting gift for a well-off chief 3 A compelling bribe for a well-off chief 4

. trade opportunities .

If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), the Manmarchers might offer the following:

  • A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus
  • A pouch full of amber (Value 3) in exchange for help getting vengeance against a rival clan or chief
  • A small herd of 6-12 tonnerhorn (Value 3 total), for 1 Surplus and help with a problem
  • An arcanum of the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VosjScKksp5B3hsc]{Green Lords}, @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.rJiU3RhbILojTC2u]{Stone Lords}, or @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{Things Below}

sections.heading: Dangers
sections.body:
Hazards

  • Nasty turn in the weather: lashing wind, bitter cold, blistering heat, sleet/ hail/rain/snow; lightning
  • Flash flooding in valleys/on slopes
  • Prairie fire on the plains
  • Mudslides and rockslides on the hills
  • Fire in a hamlet or hillfort

Monsters

  • Predators: pack drakes, cougar, feathered drakes, or wolves, maybe even a kleztigr
  • Big ornery beasts: boar, cave bears, or thunder drakes
  • Chimerae of the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.VosjScKksp5B3hsc]{Green Lords}, unearthed by recent logging or mining
  • Ghosts or revenants, lurking near old battlefields or places of bloody, one-sided slaughter
  • Emanations of the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5Kym1qDHTmM8XUQ6]{Things Below}, especially near the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.lGNIQWIn8CPHuM7E]{Dread River}
  • Spirits of the wild in the lonely, untouched places
  • Terrors come down from the @UUID[Compendium.stonetop_pwd.stonetop-journal.JournalEntry.5vFbBIn18TKtCF91]{Whitefang Mountains}

sections.body:
